content.text: A trained LoRA adapter lives alongside the frozen base model: at every forward pass the runtime computes
. That decomposition is ideal during training — only
receive gradients — but at inference it means extra matrix multiplications every layer. For a 7B model with adapters on 32 attention layers the overhead is measurable.
Merging eliminates that overhead by folding

once, yielding a single weight matrix that behaves identically but requires no adapter overhead. Beyond single-adapter deployment, merging enables combining adapters trained on different tasks into one model — a capability that has its own set of strategies depending on how many adapters are involved and how similar the tasks are.

attrs.alt: Four adapter-merging strategies: single merge into base, weighted linear (LoRA Soup), task arithmetic, and TIES merging steps

content.text: Strategy 1 — Merge a Single Adapter into the Base
is the one-liner that performs this operation. It iterates over every
parameter tensors, and returns a plain
that you save and serve like any other HuggingFace model.

from peft import PeftModel
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

base_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1",
    torch_dtype="auto",
    device_map="auto",
)

# Load the LoRA adapter on top of the base model
peft_model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base_model, "./my-lora-adapter")

# Fold ΔW = (α/r)BA into W₀ and drop the adapter layers
merged_model = peft_model.merge_and_unload()

# Save as a regular HuggingFace model
merged_model.save_pretrained("./merged-model")
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1")
tokenizer.save_pretrained("./merged-model")

# The saved model has zero adapter overhead at inference

The tradeoff is irreversibility: once merged, the fine-tuning signal is inseparable from the base weights. If you later want to swap adapters or revert to the original model behaviour you need the unmerged checkpoint. Keep the adapter directory alongside the merged model while storage is cheap.
If you want to inspect or switch adapters without permanently committing,
strips the adapter layers and returns the original frozen base model. You can then

# Switch adapters at runtime without merging into weights
peft_model.unload()                              # back to bare base model
peft_model.load_adapter("./other-adapter", adapter_name="v2")
peft_model.set_adapter("v2")
When you have several adapters trained on related tasks and want a single model that handles all of them, the simplest approach is a weighted linear combination of the update matrices:
This is sometimes called a LoRA soup by analogy with model soups in the vision literature, where averaging fine-tuned checkpoints improves generalisation. The weights
can reflect task importance, dataset size, or simply uniform averaging.

import torch
from peft import PeftModel
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM

def weighted_lora_merge(
    base_model_name: str,
    adapter_paths: list[str],
    weights: list[float],
) -> AutoModelForCausalLM:
    """
    Weighted linear merge of multiple LoRA adapters.
    All adapters must share the same base model and LoRA config.
    """
    assert abs(sum(weights) - 1.0) < 1e-6, "weights must sum to 1"
    assert len(adapter_paths) == len(weights)

    base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        base_model_name, torch_dtype=torch.float32
    )

    combined_delta: dict[str, torch.Tensor] = {}

    for path, w in zip(adapter_paths, weights):
        model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, path)
        for name, module in model.named_modules():
            if hasattr(module, "lora_A") and hasattr(module, "lora_B"):
                key = name
                scaling = module.scaling["default"]  # α/r
                dW = scaling * (module.lora_B["default"].weight
                               @ module.lora_A["default"].weight)
                if key in combined_delta:
                    combined_delta[key] += w * dW
                else:
                    combined_delta[key] = w * dW

    # Add the combined ΔW to base weights
    for name, module in base.named_modules():
        if name in combined_delta:
            module.weight.data += combined_delta[name]

    return base


merged = weighted_lora_merge(
    base_model_name="mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1",
    adapter_paths=["./adapter-task-a", "./adapter-task-b"],
    weights=[0.6, 0.4],
)
merged.save_pretrained("./soup-merged")
The practical limitation is sign interference. If adapter A pushes parameter

positive and adapter B pushes it negative, the weighted sum partially cancels both signals. For two or three closely related adapters this is usually acceptable; for many diverse tasks the TIES strategy below handles it more robustly.
Task arithmetic (Ilharco et al., 2023) generalises the soup idea beyond LoRA adapters to fully fine-tuned models. A
is the difference between a fine-tuned checkpoint and the pre-trained base:
them to remove unwanted behaviours, or scale them with a strength coefficient

parameter controls how aggressively each task vector is applied. A value near 1.0 applies the full fine-tuning shift; values below 0.5 blend conservatively towards the base. You can validate by sweeping

on a small held-out evaluation set for each task. Task arithmetic is most useful when the fine-tuned models were all trained from the same base checkpoint — divergent base models break the assumption that task vectors live in the same parameter space.
TIES (Yadav et al., 2023 — Trim, Elect Sign, and Merge) addresses the sign interference problem in weighted averaging. It operates directly on the task vectors and follows three steps:
For each adapter's task vector, keep only the top-k% of parameters by absolute magnitude; zero the rest. This removes the many small, noisy updates that would otherwise accumulate as artefacts.
For each scalar parameter, determine which sign (+/−) is held by the majority of the non-zero adapter updates. This becomes the elected sign for that position.
Average only those adapter updates that agree with the elected sign for each parameter. Updates with the opposite sign are discarded, preventing mutual cancellation.

import torch


def ties_merge(
    task_vectors: list[dict[str, torch.Tensor]],
    base_sd: dict[str, torch.Tensor],
    top_k: float = 0.2,
    lam: float = 1.0,
) -> dict[str, torch.Tensor]:
    """
    TIES merging: Trim, Elect Sign, Merge.
    task_vectors: list of {param_name: delta_tensor} dicts (θ_FT - θ_pre)
    top_k: fraction of parameters to keep (by magnitude) per adapter.
    lam: final scaling coefficient applied to the merged task vector.
    """
    merged: dict[str, torch.Tensor] = {}

    all_keys = set().union(*[tv.keys() for tv in task_vectors])

    for key in all_keys:
        deltas = [tv[key] for tv in task_vectors if key in tv]
        if not deltas:
            continue

        # Step 1 — Trim: keep top-k% by magnitude, zero the rest
        trimmed = []
        for d in deltas:
            flat = d.flatten()
            threshold = flat.abs().quantile(1.0 - top_k)
            mask = d.abs() >= threshold
            trimmed.append(d * mask)

        # Step 2 — Elect sign: majority vote per element
        stacked = torch.stack(trimmed)          # (N, *shape)
        sign_sum = stacked.sign().sum(dim=0)    # positive = more +, negative = more -
        elected_sign = sign_sum.sign()          # +1 or -1 (0 → tie, treat as +1)
        elected_sign[elected_sign == 0] = 1

        # Step 3 — Merge: average only entries that agree with elected sign
        agree_mask = (stacked.sign() == elected_sign.unsqueeze(0))  # (N, *shape)
        num_agree = agree_mask.sum(dim=0).clamp(min=1).float()
        merged_delta = (stacked * agree_mask).sum(dim=0) / num_agree

        merged[key] = base_sd[key] + lam * merged_delta

    return merged


# Example usage
base_sd = {"layer.weight": torch.zeros(4, 4)}
adapter_a = {"layer.weight": torch.randn(4, 4) * 0.1}
adapter_b = {"layer.weight": torch.randn(4, 4) * 0.1}
adapter_c = {"layer.weight": torch.randn(4, 4) * 0.1}

task_vectors = [adapter_a, adapter_b, adapter_c]
result_sd = ties_merge(task_vectors, base_sd, top_k=0.2, lam=1.0)

TIES consistently outperforms simple averaging when merging five or more diverse adapters because discarding sign-conflicting parameters prevents the interference that degrades all tasks simultaneously. The hyperparameter
(keep 20% by magnitude) is the empirically validated default from the paper; higher values retain more signal at the cost of more interference.
DARE (Yu et al., 2023) takes a probabilistic approach: randomly zero out a fraction
of the task-vector parameters, then rescale the survivors by

to keep the expected value constant. The insight is that fine-tuned models are typically dense relative to random initialisation but sparse relative to what is actually necessary — most of the weight delta can be dropped without hurting performance, and doing so reduces interference when merging.

import torch


def dare_merge(
    task_vectors: list[dict[str, torch.Tensor]],
    base_sd: dict[str, torch.Tensor],
    drop_prob: float = 0.9,
    lam: float = 1.0,
    seed: int = 42,
) -> dict[str, torch.Tensor]:
    """
    DARE: randomly prune task-vector parameters then rescale and average.
    drop_prob: fraction of parameters to zero out (0.9 works well empirically).
    """
    rng = torch.Generator()
    rng.manual_seed(seed)

    sparsified: list[dict[str, torch.Tensor]] = []
    for tv in task_vectors:
        sparse_tv: dict[str, torch.Tensor] = {}
        for key, delta in tv.items():
            mask = torch.bernoulli(
                torch.full(delta.shape, 1.0 - drop_prob), generator=rng
            ).bool()
            # Rescale survivors to keep E[sparse_tv[key]] ≈ delta
            sparse_tv[key] = (delta * mask).float() / (1.0 - drop_prob)
        sparsified.append(sparse_tv)

    # Simple average of sparsified task vectors
    merged: dict[str, torch.Tensor] = {}
    for key in base_sd:
        deltas = [tv[key] for tv in sparsified if key in tv]
        if deltas:
            avg_delta = torch.stack(deltas).mean(dim=0)
            merged[key] = base_sd[key] + lam * avg_delta
        else:
            merged[key] = base_sd[key]
    return merged
DARE is often combined with TIES (apply DARE's sparsification step first, then TIES sign election and merging), which the
method. The combination delivers the best results for 10–20 adapters covering widely different domains.
For more than a handful of adapters, the
library (arcee-ai/mergekit) provides CPU-offloaded, memory-efficient implementations of all the above strategies. You describe the merge in a YAML configuration file.

# merge_config.yaml — DARE-TIES example with 3 adapters
merge_method: dare_ties
base_model: mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1
models:
  - model: ./adapter-coding
    parameters:
      weight: 0.4
      density: 0.53     # 1 - drop_prob; keep 53% of deltas
  - model: ./adapter-math
    parameters:
      weight: 0.3
      density: 0.53
  - model: ./adapter-instruction
    parameters:
      weight: 0.3
      density: 0.53
parameters:
  normalize: true
  int8_mask: true
dtype: bfloat16
tokenizer_source: base

mergekit handles sharding, offloads layers to CPU when GPU VRAM is tight, and supports Llama, Mistral, Phi, Falcon, and other common architectures. It also implements several merge methods not covered here, including SLERP (spherical linear interpolation) and Frankenmerge (stacking layers from different models).
λ controls task strength; subtraction removes behaviours
Needs same base checkpoint for all models

Merging can silently degrade performance on any individual task. Always run a quick benchmark across all constituent tasks before deploying. A minimal script that compares the merged model against each unmerged adapter is more reliable than eyeballing generated text.

from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import pipeline
from evaluate import load as load_metric

accu = load_metric("accuracy")
pipe = pipeline("text-generation", model="./merged-output", device=0)

for task_name, dataset_name, split in [
    ("coding", "openai_humaneval", "test"),
    ("math",   "gsm8k",           "test"),
]:
    ds = load_dataset(dataset_name, split=f"{split}[:200]")
    # ... run inference and compute task-specific metric ...
    # Compare to the single-task adapter baseline for the same 200 examples
    print(f"{task_name}: merged={merged_score:.3f} single={baseline_score:.3f}")

If a task shows >5% regression relative to its single-adapter baseline, re-tune the merge weights or density for that adapter and re-run. A merge that hurts every included task is almost certainly suffering from sign interference — switch from simple averaging to TIES.
even if you plan to save in
. Accumulating deltas in low precision introduces rounding artefacts that compound across many adapters.
When merging multiple LoRA adapters (not full fine-tunes), all adapters must target the same modules with the same rank. Adapters trained with different configs produce
matrices of different shapes and cannot be summed directly.
Merging is a one-way transformation. Preserving the adapter directories costs a few hundred MB and allows re-merging with different weights later.
All adapter deltas and the base model must fit simultaneously when computing the merged weights. For 70B models, use CPU offload via mergekit's
flag or process layer by layer.
